narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared strong aspects of your academic record, including an undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this evaluation—and solid decile rankings from your early years, with an 8th decile in Year 1 (above the average of 5.71) and 6th in Year 3 (slightly above the 5.53 average). Your family falls into the middle income bracket, which is common among applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your plan to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do so compared to 93% full-time—and the model sees it as the biggest hurdle, likely associating it with divided focus or other challenges that have historically led to bar struggles. On a positive note, your Year 3 decile ranking was the second-strongest factor pulling the prediction toward success, showing the model recognized your improvement and consistency there as a promising sign. Similarly, your high Year 1 decile had a notable favorable influence, highlighting your early academic strengths as a foundation that could support passing the bar. Compared to typical students, your LSAT score of 30 falls below the average of 36.7, but it wasn't among the top drivers here; instead, the part-time status stood out as the key mismatch against the full-time norm. This combination means the model viewed your profile as riskier overall than most approved applicants, even with your solid GPA and deciles. While this rejection stings, especially given your background and achievements, it's not a judgment on your potential—many factors like part-time commitments can be managed. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, boosting your LSAT through targeted prep, or seeking programs that value diverse experiences like yours and offer bar support tailored to part-time students. You could also gather letters emphasizing your resilience and academic upward trend to strengthen future apps. Keep pushing; your story shows real promise.
